cards.grammarNote: The dual is formed by adding ـَانِ (-āni) in the nominative case. عَيْن (eye) → عَيْنَانِ (two eyes, nominative). In the genitive/accusative: عَيْنَيْنِ (-ayni). Arabic uses the dual for exactly two of anything.
يَد (yad) = hand. Dual: يَدَانِ (two hands, nominative) / يَدَيْنِ (genitive/accusative). Body parts that come in pairs naturally use the dual. When you say يَدَانِ you mean specifically two hands.

The definite dual adds الـ before the noun: الأُذُنَانِ = the two ears / both ears. The dual suffix ـَانِ remains. أُذُن (ear) is feminine, which affects adjective agreement: الأُذُنَانِ الْكَبِيرَتَانِ (the two big ears).
رِئَة (lung) is a feminine noun ending in ة (taa' marbuta). In the dual, the ة opens up to ت before the suffix: رِئَة + ـَانِ → الرِّئَتَانِ. This happens with all feminine nouns ending in ة.
After a preposition the dual takes the genitive/accusative form ـَيْنِ (-ayni): الْيَدَيْنِ (in both hands). كِلْتَا (kiltā) = both (feminine dual). For masculine: كِلَا (kilā).
عِنْدِي (ʿindī) = I have (lit. 'at me'). The dual subject عَيْنَانِ is in the nominative after عِنْدِي. Compare singular: عِنْدِي عَيْن = I have one eye.
